title: The Signal From Room Four
style_anchor: cinematic dark moody illustration, muted teal and amber palette, soft volumetric light, film grain, painterly storybook realism, 16:9, no text, no logos
character_sheet_prompt: A radio operator in his early thirties named Adam, tired eyes, short dark hair, wearing a worn navy sweater and headphones, inside a cramped Arctic research station radio room at night, glowing dials and green oscilloscope light, painterly cinematic storybook style

shots.narration: Adam took the job because it was quiet. A weather station at the edge of the Arctic, three months alone, nothing to do but log the wind and watch the snow forget the world.
shots.image_prompt: A small isolated research station half-buried in snow under a vast green aurora, a single warm window glowing in the dark Arctic night

shots.narration: The station had four rooms. A kitchen, a bunk, a storage closet, and the radio room. The previous operator had left without a word, and no one had explained why.
shots.image_prompt: Interior hallway of an old research station, four closed metal doors, flickering overhead light, frost creeping along the walls

shots.narration: For the first three weeks, nothing happened. Adam logged the temperature, ate canned soup, and listened to static on the old shortwave radio every night before sleep.
shots.image_prompt: Adam sitting alone at a cluttered radio desk, headphones on, eyes half closed, surrounded by glowing dials in a dim room

shots.narration: Then, on the twenty-second night, the static broke. Through the hiss came three slow taps, then a pause, then three taps again. Someone, somewhere, was trying to be heard.
shots.image_prompt: Close-up of a green oscilloscope screen showing three sharp pulses, Adam's startled face reflected faintly in the glass

shots.narration: Adam checked the frequency. It was a dead channel, one no station had used in decades. He tapped back, three times, the way you knock on a door. For a long moment, only static answered.
shots.image_prompt: Adam leaning forward toward the radio, finger pressing a brass telegraph key, tense concentration on his face, green light washing over him

shots.narration: And then the reply came. Three taps. Then four. Then a rhythm too deliberate to be noise. He grabbed a pencil and began to write the pattern down, his hand shaking.
shots.image_prompt: A hand writing rows of dots and dashes on a notepad under a desk lamp, the paper filling with frantic marks

shots.narration: It was Morse code. Old, slow, but unmistakable. He translated the first word, and the cold in the room seemed to deepen. The word was simple. It said: hello.
shots.image_prompt: A notepad with the word HELLO spelled faintly in the margin beside Morse marks, a coffee cup gone cold beside it

shots.narration: Adam laughed, the kind of laugh you make to push away fear. Another operator, he told himself. Some lonely ham radio enthusiast on the far side of the ice, bored like him.
shots.image_prompt: Adam smiling nervously, rubbing the back of his neck, the radio glowing in front of him in the dark room

shots.narration: So he asked a question. Where are you, he tapped, letter by letter. The answer returned almost instantly, faster than any human hand could send. It said: room four.
shots.image_prompt: The oscilloscope pulsing rapidly, Adam frozen mid-motion, his smile gone, the green light harsh on his face

shots.narration: Adam went very still. There was no other operator for a thousand miles. And the station had only four rooms. He was sitting in the radio room. Room four was the storage closet, ten steps down the hall.
shots.image_prompt: View down the dark station hallway toward a single closed door marked with a faded number four, long shadows stretching across the floor

shots.narration: He told himself it was a coincidence. A glitch. A reflection of his own signal bouncing back through old wiring. He tapped again, slower this time. Who is this, he asked.
shots.image_prompt: Adam's hands hovering over the telegraph key, knuckles white, the rest of the room swallowed in shadow

shots.narration: The reply was longer now. Letter by letter it spelled out a name, and Adam recognized it. It was the name of the operator who had come before him. The one who left without a word.
shots.image_prompt: A faded personnel photograph pinned to the wall, a man's face partly hidden by frost on the glass, dim light from the side

shots.narration: Impossible, he typed. That man went home. The radio paused, as if considering. Then it answered in a single, patient line: no one ever leaves room four.
shots.image_prompt: The storage closet door at the end of the hall, a thin line of green light leaking from underneath it into the darkness

shots.narration: Adam stood up. He told himself he would open the door, find an old transmitter left running, unplug it, and finally sleep. He took the flashlight from the desk and stepped into the hall.
shots.image_prompt: Adam standing in the hallway holding a flashlight, its beam cutting through floating dust toward the door marked four

shots.narration: The hallway felt longer than ten steps. The flashlight beam trembled. Behind him, faint and distant, he could still hear the radio tapping, even though no one was there to send.
shots.image_prompt: A narrow flashlight beam in a dark frozen corridor, the radio room glowing faintly far behind, breath visible in the cold air

shots.narration: He reached the door. It was cold enough to burn. He pressed his ear against the metal and heard, on the other side, the unmistakable sound of a telegraph key tapping all on its own.
shots.image_prompt: Adam pressing his ear to a frost-covered metal door, eyes wide, flashlight held low, tense and frightened

shots.narration: He threw the door open. The closet was empty. Only shelves, old boxes, and dust. But on a crate in the corner sat a radio set he had never seen, switched on, its dial glowing the same impossible green.
shots.image_prompt: An empty storage closet, an antique radio set glowing green on a wooden crate in the corner, dust motes in the flashlight beam

shots.narration: And beside it lay a notepad, filled in handwriting that was not his own. Page after page of the same two words, written hundreds of times, pressed so hard the pencil had torn the paper.
shots.image_prompt: A worn notepad on the floor covered edge to edge with frantic repeated handwriting, the pencil snapped in half beside it

shots.narration: The words were: let me out. Adam backed away. And the radio in the closet, the one with no power cable, no battery, nothing at all, began to tap a new message just for him.
shots.image_prompt: Close-up of the antique radio glowing in the dark with no wires attached to it, the dial trembling as if alive

shots.narration: It spelled, slowly and clearly: thank you for opening the door. He ran. Back down the hall, into the radio room, and slammed the heavy door behind him, breathing hard against the wood.
shots.image_prompt: Adam slamming the radio room door shut from inside, leaning against it, terror on his face, green light flooding the room

shots.narration: When he turned around, the main radio, his radio, was already moving. The telegraph key rose and fell by itself, tapping out a long and steady message in the empty room.
shots.image_prompt: A telegraph key moving on its own on the desk, no hand touching it, green oscilloscope pulsing behind it in the dark

shots.narration: He translated it without wanting to. Letter by letter, the room grew colder. The message was an address. A street and a number, in a city far to the south. The city where his family lived.
shots.image_prompt: Adam staring at the radio, frost spreading across the desk and dials, his breath clouding in the suddenly freezing room

shots.narration: The next morning, the relief team arrived early, ahead of schedule, because the station had stopped reporting. They found the radio room empty. The log book lay open on the desk.
shots.image_prompt: Two rescuers in heavy coats entering an empty frostbound radio room at dawn, pale grey light through the window, an open logbook on the desk

shots.narration: The final entry was written in steady hand. It said only this: the door works both ways, and now it is open. They never found Adam. But that night, a thousand miles south, a family radio began, very softly, to tap.
shots.image_prompt: A cozy dim living room far away at night, an old family radio on a shelf glowing faintly green, the rest of the house asleep
